preFix.title: Repairing a leather scuff with heat-activated compound
preFix.subtitle: Fill and colour-match a scuff or light gouge in smooth leather using a heat-set filler
preFix.excerpt: How to repair a scuff, light gouge, or small loss in smooth leather upholstery or a leather bag using a heat-activated leather filler, covering surface preparation, building up filler in layers, and applying a colour match.
preFix.sourceNotes: SATRA Technology (leather repair guidance, open access summaries). Leather Working Guild publications. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

content.text: A heat-activated leather filler is sold as part of leather repair kits intended for car interiors and furniture. These kits work equally well on bags and smaller leather goods. The technique is the same no matter the leather colour. The filler is applied in neutral form, then tinted and colour-coated on top. A scuff that has removed only the surface finish often needs only a colour coat. Not a filler pass.

Leather repair kit containing: flexible leather filler compound, colourant in a shade matching the leather, leather finish coat. Acetone or methylated spirits to degrease. Fine-grit wet-and-dry sandpaper. A small piece of foam sponge for blending colourant.
Step 1, clean and assess the damage

Clean the damaged area and 30 mm around it with acetone on a clean cloth. Removing any grease, conditioner. Or wax. Allow to dry completely. Assess the depth of the damage: if the leather fibre is exposed or lifted. Filler is needed; if only the surface coating has scuffed off and the leather beneath is smooth. Go directly to the colourant step.

Apply a thin skim of filler to the damaged area using the small palette knife or applicator supplied in the kit. Direct the heat gun at the filler from 50 to 80 mm away for 20 to 30 seconds until the surface changes from wet to matte. Allow to cool for one minute. Sand the cured layer lightly with 400-grit paper until it is flush with the surrounding leather. Wipe away sanding dust. Apply a second layer if the depression is not yet filled. Two or three thin layers give a better result than one thick layer.

Apply the leather colourant to the repair area using a small foam sponge in a dabbing motion. Blending outward into the surrounding leather to feather the edges. Allow each coat to dry before applying the next. Two or three thin coats give better coverage and adhesion than one thick coat. Check the colour match in daylight before applying the final coat; adjust by mixing colourants if needed.

Apply one or two coats of leather finish to seal and protect the colourant layer. Matching the sheen level of the surrounding leather. Allow to cure for several hours before handling. Condition the whole leather surface with a leather conditioner to restore suppleness and blend the repair into the surrounding area.

attrs.fix: Remove the failed filler. Clean and degrease thoroughly. Rebuild in thin layers, allowing each to cure before applying the next.
attrs.cause: The filler was applied too thickly in one pass, or the substrate was not degreased fully before application.
attrs.symptom: Filler cracks or flakes off after a few weeks

attrs.fix: Feather subsequent colourant coats further out onto the surrounding leather — 40 to 50 mm beyond the damage edge — so the colour transition is gradual. The larger the blend area, the less visible the repair.
attrs.cause: The colourant was applied without blending into the surrounding area, creating a hard-edged patch.
attrs.symptom: Colour match is noticeably different from the surrounding leather in certain lighting

content.text: Step-by-step instructions for Repairing a leather scuff with heat-activated compound go here.
